title: Tabbouleh

overview.description: A fresh Levantine herb salad made primarily with parsley and mint, finely chopped tomatoes, a small amount of bulgur and a bright lemon and olive oil dressing.

ingredients: 3 large bunches fresh flat-leaf parsley, finely chopped
1 large handful fresh mint leaves, finely chopped
method: Place the bulgur in a bowl.
Cover and leave for 15–20 minutes until tender.
Drain thoroughly and squeeze out any excess moisture.
Allow the bulgur to cool completely.
Finely chop the parsley and mint.
Place them in a large bowl.
Add the diced tomatoes and spring onions.
Whisk together the lemon juice, olive oil, salt and black pepper.
Pour the dressing over the salad.
Taste and adjust the lemon juice, salt and olive oil.
Leave to stand for 15–30 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: In traditional Lebanese tabbouleh, parsley is the dominant ingredient and bulgur is used in relatively small quantities. Many Western versions contain much more bulgur than is traditional.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make an herb-led salad and how lemon, olive oil and fresh herbs can create a complete dish without needing a heavy dressing.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A fresh, herb-heavy complete meal salad that provides a strong contrast to the vegetable-led Greek salad, bread-based fattoush and fish-based Niçoise.
chapman-tips: Use much more parsley than bulgur; this is the key to a more traditional style.
Dry the parsley thoroughly after washing so the salad does not become watery.
Finely chopping the herbs is important for the right texture.
Dice the tomatoes finely and drain away excess liquid if they are particularly juicy.
The lemon should be noticeable and refreshing.
Let the salad rest briefly before serving so the flavours can combine.
Tabbouleh is traditionally often eaten by scooping it up with lettuce leaves.
Don't replace the flat-leaf parsley with curly parsley if you want the most traditional flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The herbs lose freshness and the tomatoes release more liquid over time.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten within 1–2 days. Leftovers can be served in wraps or alongside hummus and falafel.
